claim: The dominant word-initial glyph constraints {o, c, q, s, d} are artifacts of a cipher mechanism that encodes plaintext vowels (a, e, i, o, u) as these five glyphs in word-initial position, with each cipher glyph mapping to a single plaintext vowel, producing a detectable co-occurrence pattern between word-initial cipher glyphs and word-final glyphs that mirrors vowel-consonant harmony in Latin or Italian.
evidence_for: Word-initial glyph constraints are strongly concentrated in just 5 glyphs, consistent with a small vowel inventory (Latin has 5 vowels, Italian 7)
If initial glyphs encode vowels, words beginning with each initial glyph should share statistical properties (length, following bigram) traceable to the underlying vowel's phonological context
Current belief [0.624] supports positional glyph constraints reflecting cipher structure
evidence_against: Latin and Italian words do not begin with vowels at the frequency implied by the dominance of these 5 initial glyphs
The cipher could instead encode consonant classes or null prefixes rather than vowels
q is almost exclusively followed by o in the Voynich text, suggesting a digraph rather than an independent vowel encoding
test: For each of the 5 dominant word-initial glyphs, compute the distribution of immediately following glyphs (second-position bigrams); test whether these second-position distributions are mutually exclusive or overlapping using a chi-squared test of independence across the 5 initial-glyph classes
test_metric: Chi-squared statistic and CramÃ©r's V for association between word-initial glyph identity and second-position glyph identity
test_threshold: CramÃ©r's V > 0.4 with p < 0.001 would confirm that initial glyphs partition into distinct phonological classes (consistent with vowel encoding); V < 0.2 would suggest initial glyphs are positional markers without phonemic specificity

tests_run.test: for each of the 5 dominant word-initial glyphs, compute the distribution of immediately following gl
tests_run.details: Positional distinctness: 0.25. First-only glyphs: {'q'}. Last-only glyphs: {'r', 'n', 'm'}. Universal glyphs: {'d', 'o', 'a', 'l'}. Strong positional constraints suggest cipher structure over grammar.

reasoning: If initial glyphs encode specific vowels, each should consistently co-occur with a predictable set of following consonant glyphs reflecting the phonotactics of the underlying language. This test can distinguish phonemically meaningful initial constraints from purely positional cipher artifacts.
